programs.name: Texas Medicaid for Long-Term Care (Elderly and People with Disabilities)
programs.tagline: Covers nursing home care and home services for parents who need daily help with bathing, dressing, or medications.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, Texas Medicaid can cover their long-term care — whether at home or in a nursing facility. This isn't regular health insurance. It's comprehensive coverage for seniors who require ongoing assistance with basic activities.

Texas offers three different paths: nursing home coverage, home and community-based services (to help your parent stay home longer), and basic medical coverage for those 65+. Because each program has different income limits and benefits, you'll need to determine which fits your parent's situation. Income limits range from $1,064/month for basic coverage to $2,982/month for nursing home care (2026).

The application process requires proving both financial need and medical necessity. Texas will assess whether your parent truly needs skilled nursing care or long-term services — meeting age and income requirements alone isn't enough.

exemptAssets: Primary residence (regardless of value for MEPD)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must require Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other basic activities) for long-term care services.
otherRequirements: Must be U.S. citizen or qualified alien
Medical necessity documentation from doctor required

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Texas HHSC or call 2-1-1 for assistance — processing time not specified by the state.

steps.title: Determine which program type applies
steps.description: Contact 2-1-1 to clarify whether your parent needs nursing home Medicaid, home-based waiver services, or basic MEPD coverage based on their care needs.

steps.title: Gather financial and medical documentation
steps.description: Collect 5 years of financial records (due to look-back rule), doctor's assessment of care needs, and proof of income, citizenship, and Texas residency.

steps.title: Submit application through HHSC
steps.description: Apply online at HHSC website, call 877-541-7905, or visit local HHSC office with all required documentation.

steps.title: Complete medical assessment
steps.description: Texas will conduct Activities of Daily Living (ADL) assessment and pre-admission screening to determine level of care needed and appropriate services.

applicationGuide.tip: Because income limits vary significantly by program type ($1,064/month for basic coverage vs $2,982/month for nursing home care), call 2-1-1 first to determine which program your parent should apply for.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Program Type
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits. HCBS Waiver limits not specified in available data.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary residence (exempt regardless of value for MEPD applicants)
Home equity up to $752,000 (for other Medicaid types if applicant intends to return)

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of citizenship or qualified alien status

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of income (Social Security, pensions, wages)
5 years of bank statements and asset transfers

categories.name: Medical
items: Doctor's assessment that parent needs assisted living or nursing facility care
Names and addresses of all medical providers

contentSections.text: Texas has a 5-year look-back rule. If your parent gifted money or sold assets below fair market value within 5 years before applying, they may face a penalty period of Medicaid ineligibility.
Your parent can keep their house regardless of value if applying for MEPD. For nursing home Medicaid, home equity must be under $752,000 if they plan to return home.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for Texas Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary residence is exempt from asset limits. For MEPD applicants, home value doesn't matter at all. For nursing home Medicaid, home equity must be under $752,000 if your mom files an 'intent to return' and no one else lives there.

faqs.question: What happens if Dad's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits vary by program type — basic MEPD coverage has a $1,064/month limit, but nursing home Medicaid allows up to $2,982/month. The research indicates pathways exist for those over financial limits, but you'll need to contact HHSC at 877-541-7905 for specifics.

faqs.question: Will the state try to recover money from Mom's estate after she passes?
faqs.answer: Yes, Texas participates in Medicaid Estate Recovery Program (MERP). The state can reclaim some Medicaid expenses from the estate of recipients who received nursing home care.

faqs.question: What if we gifted money to grandchildren in the past few years?
faqs.answer: Texas looks back 5 years at all asset transfers for nursing home Medicaid and waiver programs. Gifts or sales below fair market value can trigger a penalty period where your parent is ineligible for benefits.

faqs.question: How does Texas determine if Dad actually needs long-term care?
faqs.answer: Beyond meeting financial requirements, Texas conducts an Activities of Daily Living (ADL) assessment and pre-admission screening. Your parent must demonstrate they need daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other basic activities.

faqs.question: Which program should we apply for — there seem to be multiple types?
faqs.answer: Texas has three main programs: MEPD for basic healthcare (lowest income limits), nursing home Medicaid for facility care, and HCBS waivers for home-based services. Call 2-1-1 first to determine which fits your parent's care needs and financial situation.

stateOverview.intro: If your aging parent in Texas needs help with daily care, medication management, or bathing and dressing, Medicaid's long-term care program may cover both nursing home care and services to help them stay at home. Texas Medicaid for Long-Term Care covers these essential services for parents who meet income limits and need daily assistance with basic activities. To qualify, your parent must need the level of care typically provided in a nursing home — meaning they require daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management. Start by determining if your parent needs this level of daily assistance, as this is the key requirement that opens access to comprehensive long-term care coverage.

startHere.name: Texas Medicaid for Long-Term Care
startHere.why: Covers both nursing home care and home services if your parent needs daily help with basic activities like bathing or dressing.

byNeed.need: Daily care assistance at home or in a facility
programs: Texas Medicaid for Long-Term Care (Elderly and People with Disabilities)
byNeed.description: Covers nursing home care and home-based services for parents who need daily help with bathing, dressing, or medications.

quickFacts: Texas Medicaid for long-term care requires your parent to need 'Nursing Facility Level of Care' — meaning daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management
The program covers both nursing home placement and services to help your parent stay at home
Income and asset limits apply, but Texas allows certain exceptions for couples when only one spouse needs care
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Texas Medicaid for Long-Term Care is a qualification-based benefit — your parent must meet specific income limits and demonstrate they need daily assistance with basic activities like bathing or dressing to qualify for coverage of nursing home care or home services.
